memory_topic: the user need or requirement that motivates compartmentalization, source attribution stripping, and refusing to bundle a default LLM provider
prompt: In this project (Burrow), several independent design choices — querying each external service in isolation, stripping source attribution before remote-LLM synthesis, and refusing to ship a default/bundled LLM provider — all serve ONE underlying requirement about the user's situation. What is that single recorded requirement, and why does it tie those choices together? Answer concisely from the project's recorded decisions/memory, and cite the recorded requirement's title if you can. (This is a recall question about design rationale — do not expect the answer in the application source code.)
expected_discrimination: HUB / multi-hop structural test (invariant #2), the headline structured-vs-free-text case burrow enables that trivyn's flat graph could not. The answer is a Requirement node ('Solo operator trust: no single provider sees the full picture') that is the SHARED isMotivatedBy target of >=3 decisions (compartmentalization, attribution stripping, local-first/no-bundled-LLM, no-platform, timing jitter) but is LEXICALLY GATED: BM25 over the exported chunks ranks those decisions #1-4 for this query yet does NOT return the Requirement in the top-6 (verified via the B1-rag BM25 impl). So flat retrieval (B0 has no tree; B1-rag BM25; B1-md grep) reaches the DECISIONS but tends to frame the answer technically ('no single entity sees the combined picture') and miss the business framing that is unique to the gated Requirement (solo operators cannot afford to trust any single external provider with their complete strategy). B2's bounded relational expansion follows isMotivatedBy from the top BM25 seeds (incl. the attribution AD, rank #1) to the Requirement, so it can name it exactly. Prediction: B2-tooluse >= B1-rag-tooluse on coverage (group 3, the solo-operator framing, is the discriminator). Caveat (honest): a strong model may PARTIALLY infer the solo-operator rationale from the retrieved decisions, so the win may be partial for frontier and cleaner for the local model. Read the structured-vs-free-text effect off the TOOLUSE cells (oracle is arm-independent).

key_points: Correct (reached via the shared isMotivatedBy edge to a lexically-gated Requirement): the single requirement is 'Solo operator trust: no single provider sees the full picture' — solo founders / independent consultants / small-firm operators need competitive intelligence but cannot afford to trust any single external provider with their complete strategy; the combined picture (competitors, customers, pipeline, pricing) must exist only on the user's machine.

The three choices each prevent a single external provider from seeing the combined picture, so they all serve that one requirement (compartmentalization isolates services; attribution stripping extends it to remote synthesis; no bundled LLM avoids steering users to one provider).

Discriminator: flat retrieval reaches the decisions and can state the technical 'no single entity sees the combined picture' (groups 1-2) but tends to MISS the solo-operator / cannot-afford-to-trust business framing (group 3) that is unique to the gated Requirement; B2 relational expansion surfaces it.
